title: 第 58 節
en: IV. As to Samanya, community, or, as we should say, genus, the fourth of Kan&da's categories, it is supposed to be eternal, and a property common to several, and abiding in substance, in quality, and in action. It is distinguished by degrees, as high and low ; the highest Sam&nya, or, as we should say, the highest genus (ffati) is Satta, mere being, afterwards differentiated by Upadhis, or limitations, and developed into ever so many subordinate species. The Buddhist philosophers naturally deny the existence of such a category, and maintain that all our experience has to do with single objects only.
Visesha.

V. These single objects are what KaTi&da comprehends under his fifth category of Visesha, or that which constitutes the individuality or separateness of any object. This also is supposed to abide in eternal substances, so that it seems to have been conceived not as a mere abstraction, but as something real, that was there and could be discovered by means of analysis or abstraction.
Samavaya.

VI. The last category, with which we have met several times before, is one peculiar to Indian philosophy, Samavaya is translated by inhesion or inseparability. With Ka'ftada also it is different from mere connection, Samyoga, such as obtains between horse and rider, or between milk and water mixed together. There is Samavaya between threads and cloth, between father and son, between two halves and a whole, between cause and effect, between substances and qualities, the two being interdependent and therefore inseparable.

Though this relationship is known in non-Indian philosophies, it has not received a name of its own, though 448 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY, such a term might have proved very useful in several controversies. The relation between thought and word, for instance, is not Samyoga, but Samavaya, inseparableness.
Abb&va.

VII. In addition to these six categories, some logicians required a negative category also, that of Abhava or absence. And this also they divided into different kinds, into (i) Pragabhava, former not-being, applying to the cloth before it was woven ; (2) Dhvamsa, subsequent nonbeing, as when a jar, being smashed, exists no longer as ajar; and (3) Atyantabhava, absolute not-being, an impossibility, such as the son of a barren woman ; (4) Anyonyabhava, reciprocal negation, or mutual difference, such as we see in the case of water and ice.

It may seem as if the Vaiseshika was rather a disjointed and imperfect system. And to a certain extent it is so. Though it presupposes a knowledge of the Nyaya-system, it frequently goes over the same ground as the Nyaya, though it does not quote verbatim from it. We should hardly imagine that the Vaiseshika- Stitras would argue against Upam&na, or comparison, as a separate Prama^ia, in addition to Pratyaksha (sense) and Anum&na (inference), unless in some other school it had been treated as an independent means of knowledge ; and this school was, as we saw, the ISTyaya, which is so far shown to be anterior to the Vaiseshika-philosophy. Ka?iada denies by no means that comparison is a channel through which knowledge may reach us, he only holds that it is not an independent channel, but must be taken as a subdivision of another and larger channel, viz. Anum&na or inference. He probably held the same opinion about /Sabda, whether we take it in the sense of the Veda or of an utterance of a recognised authority, because the recognition of such an authority always implies, as he rightly holds, a previous inference to support it. He differs in this respect from the Jiarvaka secularist, who denies the authority of the Vecla outright, while KaTiada appeals to it in several places.

A similar case meets us in Gotarna's Nyaya-Sutras (1, 16). Here, apparently without any definite reason, Gotama tells THE SIX SYSTEMS. 445 us in a separate aphorism that Buddhi (understanding), Upalabdhi (apprehension), and CWana (knowing) are not different in meaning. Why should he say so, unless he had wanted to enter his protest against some one else who had taught that they meant different things ? Now this some one else could only have been Kapila, who holds, as we saw, that Buddhi is a development of Prakrit! or unintelligent nature, and that conscious apprehension (Samvid) originates with the Purusha only. But here again, though Gotama seems to have had the tenets of the SSmkhyaschool in his eye, we have no right on this ground to say that our Samkhya-Sfttras existed before the Ny&ya-Sutras were composed. All we are justified in saying is that, like all the other systems of Indian philosophy, these two also emerged from a common stratum in which such opinions occupied the minds of various thinkers long before the final outcome settled down, and was labelled by such names as SUmkhya, or Nyaya, Kapila, or Gotama, and long, of course, before the Samkhya-Sutras, which we now possess, were constructed.
The Six Systems.

It must have been observed how these six, or, if we include the B&rhaspatya, these seven systems of philosophy, though they differ from each other and criticise each other, share nevertheless so many things in common that we can only understand them as products of one and the same soil, though cultivated by different hands. They all promise to teach the nature of the soul, and its relation to the Godhead or to a Supreme Being. They all undertake to supply the means of knowing the nature of that Supreme Being, and through that knowledge to pave the way to supreme happiness. They all share the conviction that there is suffering in the world which is something irregular, has no right to exist, and should therefore be removed. Though there is a strong religious vein running through the six so-called orthodox systems, they belong to a phase of thought in which not only has the belief in the many Yedic gods long been superseded by a belief in a Supreme Deity, such as Pra^pati, but this phase also has been left behind Gg 45O INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

to make room for a faith in a Supreme Power, or in the Godhead which has no name but Brahman or Sat, * I am what I am.' The Hindus themselves make indeed a distinction between the six orthodox systems. They have no word for orthodox; nay, we saw that some of these systems, though atheistic, were nevertheless treated as permissible doctrines, because they acknowledged the authority of the Veda. Orthodox might therefore be replaced by Vedic ; and if atheism seems to us incompatible with Vedism or Vedic orthodoxy, we must remember that atheism with Indian philosophers means something very different from what it means with us. It means a denial of an active, busy, personal or humanised god only, who is called Isvara, the Lord. But behind him and above him Hindu philosophers recognised a Higher Power, whether they called it Brahman, or Param&tman, or Purusha. It was the denial of that reality which constituted a N&stika, a real heretic, one who could say of this invisible, yet omnipresent Being, Na asti, c He is not.' Buddha therefore, as well as Bnhaspati, the JT&rvaka, was a N&stika, while both, the Yoga and the Samkhya, the former Sesvara, with an Isvara, the other Anisvara, without an Isvara, the one theistic, the other atheistic, could be recognised as orthodox or Vedic.

The Hindus themselves were fully aware that some of their systems of philosophy differed from each other on essential points, and that some stood higher than others. Madhusudana clearly looked upon the Vedanta as the best of all philosophies, and so did $amkara, provided he was allowed to interpret the Sfttras of Badar&ya^ia according to the principles of his own unyielding Monism. Madhusudana, as we saw, treated the S&mkhya and Yoga by themselves as different from the two Mimamsas, Ny&ya and Vaiseshika, and as belonging to Smriti rather than to 8jro.ii. Vigrw&na-Bhikshu, a philosopher of considerable grasp, while fully recognising the difference between the six systems of philosophy, tried to discover a common truth behind them all, and to point out how they can be studied together, or rather in succession, and how all of them are meant to lead honest students into the way of truth.

THE SIX SYSTEMS, 451 In his Preface to the S&mkhya-Sfttras, so well edited and translated by Professor Garbe, Vi(/ttana-Bhikshu says : " If we read in the Brihadara^yaka Upanishad II, 4, 5, and IV, 5, 6, that the Self must be seen, must be heard, must be pondered and meditated on, hearing and the rest are evidently pointed out as means of a direct vision of the Self, by which the highest object of man can be realised. If it is asked how these three things can be achieved, Smriti or tradition answers : ' It must be heard from the words of the Veda, it must be pondered on with proper arguments, and, after that, it must be meditated on continuously. These are the means of the vision of the Self/ ' Meditated on/ that is, by means proposed in Yogaphilosophy. Three things are known from passages of the Veda, (i) the highest object of man, (2) jmowledge essential for its attainment, (3) the nature of the Atman or Self which forms the object of such knowledge. And it was the purpose of the Exalted, as manifested in the form of Kapila, to teach, in his six-chaptered manual on Viveka or distinction between Purusha and Prakriti, all the arguments which are supported by $ruti.

If then it should be objected that we have already a logical treatment of these subjects in the Nyaya and Vaiseshika systems, rendering the Samkhya superfluous, and that it is hardly possible that both — the Samkhya as well as the Ny&ya and Vaiseshika — could be means of right knowledge, considering that each represents the Self in a different form, the Ny&ya and Vaiseshika as with qualities, the Samkhya as without, thus clearly contradicting each other, we answer No, by no means ! Neither is the S&mkhya rendered superfluous by the Nyaya and Vaiseshika, nor do they contradict each other. They differ from each other so far only as Nyaya and Vaiseshika treat of the objects of empirical knowledge, but the S&mkhya of the highest truth. The Ny&ya and Vaiseshika, as they follow the common-sense view that it is the Self that feels joy and pain, aim at no more than at the first steps in knowledge, namely at the recognition of the Atman as different from the body, because it is impossible to enter per saltum into the most abstruse wisdom. The knowledge of those 452 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

preliminary schools which is attained by simply removing the idea that the Self is the body is no more than an empirical comprehension of facts, in the same manner as by a removal of the misapprehension in taking a man at a distance for a post, there follows the apprehension that he has hands, feet, &c., that is, a knowledge of the truth, yet purely empirical. If therefore we read the following verse from the Bhagavad-git& III, 29 : —

( Those who are deceived by the constituent GuTias of Prakriti, cling to the workings of the Gmias (Sattva, Ra#as, and Tamas). Let therefore those who know the whole truth take care not to distract men of moderate understanding who do not as yet know the whole truth ; ' — we see that here the followers of the Nyaya and Vaiseshika systems, though they hold to the false belief that the Self can be an agent, are not treated as totally in error, but only as not knowing the whole truth, if compared with the S&mkhyas, who know the whole truth. Even such knowledge as they possess, leads step by step by means of the lower impassiveness (Apara-vairagya) to liberation ; while the knowledge of the Samkhyas only, as compared with the lower knowledge, is absolute knowledge, and leads by means of higher impassiveness (Paravair&gya) straight to liberation. For it follows from the words quoted from the Bhagavad-gita that he only who knows that the Self is never an agent, can arrive at the whole truth, and from hundreds of true Vedic texts, such as Brih. Ar. Up. IV, 3, 22: 'Then he has overcome all the sorrows of the heart'; thinking that desires, &e., belong to the internal organ (Manas) only ; or Brih. Ar. Up. IV, 3, 7 : 1 He, remaining the same (the Self), wanders through both worlds, as^ if thinking, and as if moving (but not really) ' ; or BHh. Ar. Up. IV, 3, 16: 'And whatever he may have seen there he is not followed (affected) by it ' ; and likewise from hundreds of similar passages in the Snm'ti, such as Bhag. Ill, 27 : ' All works are performed by the constituents of matter (the Gunas of Prakriti) ; he only who is deceived by Ahamkara or subjectivation imagines that he is the agent'; and such as V. P. VI, 7, 22: 'The Self consists of bliss (Nirvfiwa) and knowledge only, and is not con- THE SIX SYSTEMS. 453 taminated (by the Giwas). The qualities ( GUT? as) are full of suffering, not of knowledge, and they belong to Prakriti, not to the Self ' — from all such passages we say that it is clear that the knowledge proclaimed by Nyaya and Vaiseshika with regard to the highest subject is overcome.

By this, however, we do not mean to say that Nyaya and Vaiseshika are not means of right knowledge, for their teaching is not superseded by the Samkhya so far as regards that portion which treats of the difference between Self and the material body. Here we must follow the principle (laid down in the Purva-Mimamsa), that what a word (chiefly) aims at, that is its meaning ; (and apply it to the systems of philosophy). The Ny&ya simply repeats the popular idea that joy pertains to the Self, without referring to any further proofs ; and this chapter therefore is not to be considered as really essential (or as what the Nyaya chiefly aims at).

But admitting that there is here no difference between Ny&ya-Vaiseshika and the Samkhya systems, is there not a clear contradiction between the Samkhya on one side and the Brahma-Mimamsa (Vedanta) and the Yoga on the other? The former denies the existence of an eternal tsvara, the two others maintain it. Surely it cannot be said that here also the contradiction between these systems, the atheistic and theistic, can be removed by simply admitting, as before, two points of view, the metaphysical and the empirical, as if the theistic doctrine existed only for the sake of the worship of the multitude. Such a decision would here be impossible. The atheistic view that an Isvara is difficult to know and therefore non-existent, may well have been merely repeated by the Samkhyas, as a popular idea, and in order to put an end to the desire of men for acquiring a divine status and divine honours (by means of penance, &c.), as in the case of the Naiyayikas when they say that the Self possesses qualities (which must be taken as merely a provisional remark). In the Veda or elsewhere Isvara, the anthropomorphic deity, is never explicitly denied, so that one could say that theism should be taken as the common popular view only.
In spite of all this we hold that here too these different 454 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.
views are really due to empirical or to metaphysical conceptions.
For as works like the Bhagavad-gita (XVI, 8) when saying : —

' Those say that the world is unreal, without support, without an tsvara/ condemn the atheistic doctrine, we may very well suppose that the S&mkhyas simply repeated a common popular view that there is no Isvara, in order to discourage the striving after a divine status (so common among Saints), or for some similar purpose. They would naturally think that if they, so far f olio wing^ the materialists, did not deny the existence of an active Isvara, the acquisition of the discriminating knowledge (of the Samkhyas, between Prakrit! and Purusha) would be impeded, because those who believe in an infinite, eternal and perfect Isvara, have their thoughts entirely absorbed by this Isvara (so that they might not attend to the essential doctrine of the Samkhyas). No attack is made anywhere on theism, so that the theistic doctrine of the Vedanta should be restricted to sacrificial and similar purposes only. But from passages like Mahabh. XII, 1167: 'No knowledge is equal to that of the Samkhya, no power to that of the Yoga/ and again XII, 1 1198 : cLet there be no doubt, the knowledge of the Samkhya is considered the highest/ we should learn the excellence of the Samkhya knowledge as superior to other systems, though only with regard to that portion which treats of the distinction of Self and Prakriti, and not with regard to the portion that objects to an Isvara. Furthermore from the consensus of Parasara also and all other eminent authorities, we see that theism alone is absolutely true. And from Par&sara's Upa-pura?ia and similar works the truth of the Brahma-Mim&ms& in its chapter on the Isvara is perfectly manifest. There we read : —

' In the systems of Akshapada and Ka^ada (Nyaya and Vaiseshika), in the Samkhya and in the Yoga, whatever portion is in conflict with the Veda, that has to be rejected by all to whom the Veda is the only law/ ' In the systems of ffaiinini and Vy&sa (in the Purva and Uttara-Mimgimsa) there is nothing in conflict with the THE SIX SYSTEMS. 455 Veda; for these two in their knowledge o£ the meaning of the Veda have by means of the Veda fully mastered the Veda.'
From other passages also the superior authority of the Brahma-Mim&msa may be gathered, at least with regard to that portion which treats of Isvara. Thus we read in Mahabh. XII, 7663 seq. :—

' Manifold philosophical doctrines have been propounded by various teachers ; but cling to that only which has been settled by arguments, by the Veda, and by the practice of good people/ From this passage of the Mokshadharma also (XII, 7663), and on account of the practice of Par&sara and all eminent authorities, it follows that the proof of the existence of an tsvara, as proclaimed by the Brahma-Mimamsa, the Nyaya, Vaiseshika and other systems, is to be, accepted as the strongest ; and likewise because by passages in the Kurma and other PuraTias the ignorance of the S&^khyas with regard to an tsvara has been clearly pronounced by NarayaTia and others; e.g. 'Take thy refuge with the beginningiess and endless Brahman, whom the Samkhyas, though strong as Yogins, are unable to perceive/ Besides, that Isvara alone is the principal object of the Brahma-Mimamsa is proved by the very first words and by other indications. If then it had been refuted on that principal point, the whole philosophy (the BrahmaMimamsa) would no longer be a means of right knowledge, according to the principle, mentioned before, that what a word chiefly aims at, that is its meaning. The chief aim of the Samkhya, on the contrary, is not the denial of an Isvara, but the highest object to be obtained by the Self by means of the discrimination between body and Self which leads to it. Hence, though it be superseded in that part which treats of the denial of the Isvara, it will remain as a means of right knowledge, and this once more according to the principle that what a word chiefly aims at, that is its purport. The Samkhya has therefore its proper sphere, and is vulnerable in that part only which treats of the denial of the Isvara, the personal and active god.
Nor would it be right to say that in the Brahma-Mimamsa Isvara may indeed be the principal object, but not its 456 INDIAN PHILOSOPHY.

eternal lordship or godhead. For, as the objection raised in the Purvapaksha as to its (the Mimamsa's) allowing no weight to the other Snmtis cannot be sustained, it is clear that Isvara can only be the object of the Brahma-Mimamsa, provided he is characterised by eternal lordship.

If it is said that the first Sfttra of the Brahma-Mimamsa does not say 'Now then a wish to know the highest Brahman,' and that therefore it does not by the word Brahman mean the Parabrahman, we must not on account of the Samkhya denial of an Isvara suppose that the Vedanta and Yoga systems likewise refer only to an evolved Isvara (a Karyesvara, a product of Prakriti), for in that case the whole string of Sutras from II, 3, i, directed against the Samkhya and showing that mindless matter, being incapable of creating, cannot be established by mere reasoning, would be absurd ; for if the God of the Vedanta were a made God, or a product of matter, the S&mkhyas would have been right in teaching an independent matter (Prakriti). Lastly, the eternal character of Isvara is quite clear from such Yoga-Sutras as I, 26, < He (God) is the Guru even of the oldest sages, because he is not limited by time,' and likewise from Vy&sa's commentary on that Sutra. It is clear therefore that as the S&mkhya means to deny the common popular anthropomorphic view of Isvara only, whether as a concession, or as a bold assertion, or, for some other reason, there exists no real contradiction between it, and the Brahma-Mim&msa, and the Yoga.
zh: IV. 關於薩摩若亞（Samanya），即「共性」或如我們所說的「屬」，這是堪陀第四個範疇，它被認為是永恆的，是存在於多個事物中的共同性質，內在於實體、性質與作用之中。它按程度而有所區別，有高低之分；最高的薩摩若亞，或如我們所說的最高的屬（jati）是薩塔（Satta），即純粹的「有」，其後由烏帕笛斯（Upadhis）即「限定」而得以分化，並發展成無數的下位物種。佛教哲學家自然否定這樣的範疇的存在，並主張我們的一切經驗都只與單個對象有關。
維舍沙（Visesha）。
五、這些單一的對象就是迦底羅（KaTi&da）在其第五個範疇「殊性」（Visesha）之下所理解的內容，即構成任何對象的個別性或分殊性的那個東西。這也被認為是存在於永恆實體之中的，因此似乎被構想為不僅是純粹的抽象物，而是某種真實的東西，它本來就在那裡，並且可以通過分析或抽象的手段被發現。
三昧耶（Samavaya）。
VI. 最後一個範疇，是我們之前多次遇到的，也是印度哲學特有的。薩摩婆耶（Samavaya）譯為內屬性或不可分離性。據卡那陀（Ka'ftada）之說，它異於純粹的連合（Samyoga），譬如馬與騎士之間、或牛乳與水混合在一起所具有的那種連合。線與布、父與子、二分與整體、原因與結果、實質與性質之間存在著薩摩婆耶，此二者相互依存，因此不可分離。
雖然這種關係在非印度哲學中亦為人所知，然而它並未獲得專屬的名稱，儘管這樣的術語本可在諸多論爭中被証明為甚為有用。例如思想與言詞之間的關係，並非 Samyoga（相合），而是 Samavaya（內在不可分性）。
我已準備好協助翻譯，但我注意到您提供的英文原文只有「Abb&va.」這個片段，這似乎是個不完整或縮寫的文本。 請問您能提供完整的英文原文供我翻譯嗎？這樣我才能按照您詳細的規則進行專業的繁體中文翻譯。

VII. 除了這六個範疇之外，某些邏輯學家還要求加入否定範疇，即無（Abhava）。他們並將其分為不同的種類：（i）先無（Pragabhava），指事物未成之前的不存在狀態，如布料在織造之前；（2）後無（Dhvamsa），指事物已毀之後的不存在狀態，如陶罐被打碎後不復存在為罐；（3）絕對無（Atyantabhava），不可能的事物，如貧婦之子；（4）相互否定（Anyonyabhava），即相互差異，如我們在水與冰的情況中所見。

吠世史迦系統似乎是相當零碎且不完整的。在某種程度上確實如此。雖然它預設了尼夜耶（Nyaya）系統的知識，它卻經常涉及與尼夜耶相同的領域，儘管並未逐字引用。除非在某個其他學派中，比較（Upamâna）已被視為獨立的認識手段，否則我們難以想像吠世史迦經（Vaiseshika-Sûtras）會論證反對比較作為一種獨立的量（Pramâna），此外還有現量（Pratyaksha）（感官知覺）與比量（Anumâna）（推論）。如我們所見，這個學派就是尼夜耶，它因此被證明是先於吠世史迦哲學的。迦那陀（Ka‌ṇâda）絕非否認比較是知識可以到達我們的管道，他只是主張它不是獨立的管道，而必須視為另一個且更廣大管道的細分，即比量（Anumâna）或推論。他對於「聲」（Śabda）——無論我們將其理解為吠陀或認可權威的言說——很可能持相同看法，因為對這樣一個權威的認可始終隱含著，如他正確地主張的，一個先前的推論來支持它。他在這一點上不同於竭婆迦（Charvaka）世俗派，後者直言否定吠陀的權威，而迦那陀則在多處訴諸於它。

我們在高塔瑪（Gotama）的《尼耶亞經文》（Nyaya-Sutras）（1, 16）中遇到類似的情況。在這裡，高塔瑪似乎沒有任何明確的理由，在一條獨立的格言中告訴我們**六大系統** 445 ，說覺慧（Buddhi）、覺知（Upalabdhi）和知識（Jnana）在意義上並無區別。除非他想對某個教導它們意義不同的人提出異議，否則為何要這樣說呢？這某個人只能是迦毘羅（Kapila），我們已經看到他主張覺慧是原質（Prakrit）或非智性自然的一個展開，而有意識的覺知（Samvid）只源於神我（Purusha）。但是在這裡，雖然高塔瑪似乎已將數論派（Samkhya school）的教義納入考量，我們卻沒有權利基於此而說我們現有的《數論經文》（Samkhya-Sutras）成書於《尼耶亞經文》之前。我們所能正當地說的只是，與印度哲學的所有其他系統一樣，這兩個系統也都源於一個共同的基層，在這個基層中，各種思想家長期以來就心懷著這類主張，遠早於其最終成果得以確定並被冠以數論或尼耶亞、迦毘羅或高塔瑪等名號的時代，而當然也遠早於我們現在所擁有的那些《數論經文》被編纂的年代。

在印度哲學中，我們發現有六種正統體系，這些體系都承認吠陀的權威性。這六大體系通常按對偶形式配對： 1. **衛世史（Sânkhya）與瑜伽（Yoga）** — 衛世史是一套唯物論的哲學體系，而瑜伽則是其實踐方法。 2. **正理（Nyâya）與勝論（Vaisheshika）** — 正理關涉於邏輯與認識論，勝論則論述事物的本質。 3. **彌曼差（Mîmânsâ）與韋檀多（Vedânta）** — 彌曼差專注於吠陀儀式的詮釋，韋檀多則關乎吠陀的玄祕教義與最高的精神真理。 這六大體系雖各有其獨特之處，但皆以吠陀為權威基礎，並致力於闡明終極實在的本質。它們代表了印度思想傳統中理性與宗教思辨的最高成就，並對後世的印度文明與宗教實踐產生了深遠的影響。

必然可以觀察到，這六個，或者如果我們將婆羅訶斯跋多耶派（Bṛhaspatya）包括在內，這七個哲學體系，雖然彼此不同且互相批評，但它們卻共享許多共同之處，以至於我們只能將它們理解為出自同一土壤的產物，只是由不同的人手所耕耘罷了。它們都承諾教導靈魂的本質，以及靈魂與神性或至高存在者的關係。它們都著手供應認識那至高存在者本質的手段，並通過那種知識為達至至高幸福鋪路。它們都共享這樣的信念：世界上存在著痛苦，這是某種不規則的東西，沒有存在的理由，因此應該被消除。儘管在這六個所謂正統的體系中有著強烈的宗教脈絡貫穿其中，但它們屬於思想的一個階段，在這個階段不僅對眾多吠陀諸神的信仰早已被對至高神祇（如婆羅波提）的信仰所取代，而且這一階段本身也已被超越。

為無限者騰出空間，或為那沒有名字、只被稱作梵天（Brahman）或有（Sat）的神性騰出空間，*「我是我所是」。印度教徒本身確實在六大正統派系之間做出區分。他們沒有「正統」這個詞；更確切地說，我們看到其中某些派系雖然是無神論的，卻仍然被視為許可的學說，因為他們承認吠陀的權威。因此，「正統」一詞或可改稱為「吠陀的」；若無神論在我們看來與吠陀主義或吠陀正統性不相容，我們必須記住，無神論在印度哲學家那裡的意義與在我們這裡大不相同。它只意指對一個活躍的、好干預的、個人化的或擬人化的神的否定，這樣的神被稱作伊濕瓦羅（Isvara），即「主宰」。但在他之後、之上，印度哲學家們認識到更高的力量，無論他們稱之為梵天（Brahman）、至高自我（Paramatman）或原人（Purusha）。否定這個現實的行為構成了一個「否定者」（Nastika），一個真正的異端者，他可以對這個看不見卻無所不在的存有說「他不存在」（Na asti）。因此，佛陀（Buddha）與同樣是無神論者的雨行者婆羅賀斯帕蒂（Brhaspati, the Charvaka）都是否定者，而瑜伽派與數論派則都可被認定為正統的或吠陀的——前者是有主神的（Sesvara，有伊濕瓦羅），後者是無主神的（Anisvara，無伊濕瓦羅），一個有神論的，一個無神論的。

印度人本身完全意識到他們某些哲學體系在根本要點上相互不同，且某些體系高於其他體系。馬杜蘇達納（Madhusudana）明確地將吠檀多視為所有哲學中最優越者，商羯羅（Samkara）亦然，惟其前提是被允許按照自身不可動搖的一元論原則來詮釋巴達衍闍（Badarayana）的經文（Sfttras）。如我們所見，馬杜蘇達納將數論派（Samkhya）與瑜伽派（Yoga）視為有別於兩種密摩罕薩派（Mimamsas）、尼耶亞派（Nyaya）與瓦伊舍什卡派（Vaiseshika）者，並將其歸於《博聞錄》（Smriti）而非《吠陀》（Sruti）。毗格瓦納‧比丘（Vignana-Bhikshu）是一位視野宏闊的哲學家，在充分認識六大哲學體系之差異的同時，嘗試發掘隱蘊於它們之後的共同真理，並指出如何能夠將它們一併研習，或毋寧說依序研習，以及它們如何皆旨在引領誠實的求學者進入真理之途。

在由葛貝教授（Professor Garbe）精心編譯的《數論經》（Sāṃkhya-Sūtras）序文中，毘耶陀婆笈多（Vīttana-Bhikṣu）說道：「如果我們在《廣林奧義書》（Brihadāraṇyaka Upaniṣhad）II, 4, 5 及 IV, 5, 6 中讀到『自我（Self）必須被看見，必須被聽聞，必須被思考且被冥想』，那麼聽聞等明顯被指出為直接觀照自我的手段，藉此人類的最高目標可以被實現。如果有人問這三件事如何能夠達成，傳統（Smriti）回答道：『它必須從吠陀（Veda）的言辭中被聽聞，它必須以適當的論證被思考，之後它必須被持續地冥想。這些是自我之視見的手段。』『被冥想』，亦即藉由瑜伽哲學所提議的手段。三件事從吠陀（Veda）的段落中被認知：（1）人類的最高目標，（2）達成其獲得所需的知識，（3）作為此種知識對象的阿特曼（Atman）或自我的本性。並且，以卡皮拉（Kapila）形式顯現的至尊者的目的，就是在他那部關於毗婆訶（Viveka）或普魯沙（Purusha）與普拉克里蒂（Prakṛti）之間區分的六章手冊中，教導所有由聖論（śruti）所支持的論證。」

倘若有人反駁說，我們已經在尼夜耶（Nyaya）與瓦伊舍希卡（Vaiseshika）體系中具有對這些主題的邏輯論述，這使得數論派（Samkhya）顯得多餘，而且考慮到二者——數論派以及尼夜耶與瓦伊舍希卡派——各自以不同的形式代表自我，尼夜耶與瓦伊舍希卡派視自我為具有性質的，數論派視之為無性質的，因此明顯相互矛盾，雙方都不可能作為正知的手段，我們的回答是：絕非如此！數論派並未被尼夜耶與瓦伊舍希卡派所削弱，他們彼此間亦不相互矛盾。他們的區別僅在於：尼夜耶與瓦伊舍希卡派論述的是經驗知識的對象，而數論派論述的是最高的真理。尼夜耶與瓦伊舍希卡派遵循常識性的觀點，即自我會感受喜樂與痛苦，因此其目標不過是知識的初步階段，即認識到阿特曼（Atman）異於身體，因為不可能一步登天地進入最深奧難懂的智慧。那些知識 452 印度哲學。
初級學派所達成的境界，僅係藉由除卻「自我即肉身」之觀念而得，不過是對事實之經驗性認識而已。其情形猶如藉由消除「將遠處之人誤認為木樁」之誤解，隨之而來對其具有手、足等特徵之認識，亦即真理之知識，然而純屬經驗性。因此若我們閱讀《薄伽梵歌》(Bhagavad-gīta)第三章第 29 節之下列詩句：—

那些被實質（Prakriti）的成分所迷惑的人，執著於成分的作用（Gunas——薩埵、羅闍和塔摩）。因此，讓那些知曉全部真理的人小心，不要使那些尚未知曉全部真理的中等理解力的人分心；——我們看到，雖然遵循尼耶亞（Nyaya）和瓦依舍希卡（Vaiseshika）體系的人堅持自我可以是行動者的錯誤信念，但他們並非被視為完全錯誤，而只是被視為尚未知曉全部真理，相比之下，知曉全部真理的數論派（Samkhyas）則有所不同。即使他們所擁有的知識，也能通過較低的離欲（Apara-vairagya）逐步引向解脫；而相比之下，數論派的知識作為較高的知識，乃是絕對知識，能通過較高的離欲（Paravairagya）直接通向解脫。這是因為從《薄伽梵歌》（Bhagavad-gita）引用的詞句可以看出，只有知道自我決不是行動者的人，才能達到全部真理；而且從數百篇真實的吠陀文獻亦可看出，例如《大森林奧義書》（Brih. Ar. Up.）IV, 3, 22：「然後他已克服心中所有的悲傷」；認為欲望等只屬於內器（Manas）；或《大森林奧義書》IV, 3, 7：「他，保持不變（自我），遊歷於兩個世界，彷彿思想，彷彿移動（但並非真實）」；或《大森林奧義書》IV, 3, 16：「無論他在那裡看到什麼，他都不受其影響」；同樣地，從《史詩》（Smriti）中數百條類似的段落，例如《薄伽梵歌》III, 27：「所有的行為都是由物質的成分（實質的成分）進行的；只有那些被自我中心化（Ahamkara）所迷惑的人才想像自己是行動者」；以及《毗濕奴往世書》（V. P.）VI, 7, 22：「自我僅由極樂（Nirvana）和知識組成，且不被成分所玷污。成分（Gunas）充滿苦難，而非知識，它們屬於實質，不屬於自我」——從所有這些段落，我們說明顯可知，尼耶亞和瓦依舍希卡關於最高主題所宣揚的知識已被超越。

由此我們不是要說明紐亞耶（Nyaya）與毗世師迦（Vaiseshika）不是正知的手段，因為在涉及自我與物質身體之間差異的部分，三陀迦耶（Samkhya）並未將其教義推翻。在此我們必須遵循先行彌曼差（Purva-Mimamsa）所奠定的原則，即一個詞（語言）（chiefly）意欲指向什麼，那就是它的意義；（並將之應用於哲學體系）。紐亞耶只是重申了通俗觀念，即快樂屬於自我，但未涉及任何進一步的證明；因此這一章不應被視為真正本質之處（或被視為紐亞耶所主要意欲的內容）。

但假如我們承認此處尼亞耶-毗世師迦系統與數論系統之間並無差異，那麼數論系統與一方面的梵我論（韋檀多）及瑜伽系統另一方面之間，豈不是存在著明顯的矛盾嗎？前者否定永恆伊濕瓦羅（Isvara）的存在，後兩者則堅持其存在。誠然不能說，此處這些系統之間的矛盾——無神論的與有神論的——也能夠藉由簡單地承認如前所述的兩個觀點（形上學的觀點與經驗的觀點）而消除，彷彿有神論的教義僅為多數民眾的崇拜而存在。這樣的決定在此是不可能的。無神論觀點認為伊濕瓦羅難以認知，因此不存在，這種看法或許不過是被數論者當作一種通俗觀念而重複提出的，目的在於終止人類獲得神聖地位與神聖榮譽的欲望（藉由苦行等手段），就如同尼亞耶學派所言，自我具有種種品質（這應當被視為單純的權宜之計）。在《吠陀》或其他地方，伊濕瓦羅這位擬人化的神，從未被明確否定，因此可以說有神論應當被視為普遍的通俗觀點而已。
儘管如此，我們認為在這裡同樣地這些不同
這些觀點究竟源於經驗性的或形上學的概念。
因為像《薄伽梵歌》（XVI, 8）這樣的著作在說述時：—

那些人說世界是非實的、無所憑依、無有伊濕瓦羅（Isvara）。我們譴責無神論教義時，可以很合理地推測，數論派（Samkhyas）僅是重複一種普遍流行的觀點，即不存在伊濕瓦羅，目的是為了阻止尋求神聖地位的努力（這在聖者中極為普遍），或出於類似目的。他們自然會認為，倘若他們在某些方面跟隨唯物論者，不否定活動的伊濕瓦羅之存在，則數論派的區別知識的獲得（即原質與神我之間的區別）將受阻礙，因為那些相信無限、永恆、完美伊濕瓦羅的人，其思想完全為此伊濕瓦羅所吸收（以至於他們可能無法專注於數論派的基本教義）。任何地方都未對有神論進行攻擊，使得吠檀多派的有神論教義應當僅限於祭祀等目的。但從《摩訶婆羅多》第十二卷1167節的經文：「無知識等於數論之知識，無力量等於瑜伽之力量」，以及同卷1198節：「毫無疑問，數論之知識被視為最高」，我們應該認識到數論知識優於其他體系的卓越性，儘管僅就涉及自我與原質之區別的部分而言，而非關於反對伊濕瓦羅之部分。此外，從帕拉沙羅及所有其他傑出權威的共識來看，我們可見唯有有神論才是絕對真實的。從帕拉沙羅的《往世書補編》（Upa-purā?ia）及類似著作，我們可看出梵我論（Brahma-Mimā?sa）關於伊濕瓦羅一章的真理完全昭然若揭。我們在那裡讀到：——
在阿剎婆陀（Akshapada）和迦那陀（Ka^ada）的體系（正理論和勝論）、數論和瑜伽中，凡與吠陀相衝突之處，對於以吠陀為唯一法則的人們來說，必須加以棄絕。在跋衣尼（Pānini）和毘耶娑（Vyāsa）的體系中（在前彌曼差和後彌曼差），與吠陀毫無衝突之處；因為這兩者在其對吠陀意義的知識中，已經透過吠陀而徹底掌握了吠陀。
從其他段落也可以看出梵天彌曼差（Brahma-Mim&msa）的卓越權威性，至少就其涉及伊濕瓦羅（Isvara）的部分而言。因此我們在《摩訶婆羅多》（Mahabh.）XII, 7663 及後續章節中讀到：—

「多種哲學教義已由各派導師所宣揚；但應當依循唯有通過論證、吠陀與善良之人之實踐所確定者。」由《解脫法》(Mokshadharma)之此段落(XII, 7663)亦然，且因帕若薩羅(Parasara)與所有傑出權威人士之實踐，故可推知梵經論(Brahma-Mimamsa)、尼耶亞派(Nyaya)、瓦希什迦派(Vaiseshika)及其他體系所宣述之伊濕瓦羅(Isvara)存在之証明應被接受為最強有力者；同樣地，因《龜經》(Kurma)與其他往世書(Purana)中之段落已由那羅衍那(Narayana)及他人明確地宣言數論派(Samkhyas)關於伊濕瓦羅之無知，例如「以無始無終之梵(Brahman)為汝之庇護所，即使強如瑜伽士之數論派亦無法認知者。」除此，伊濕瓦羅獨為梵經論之主要對象此事已由該派之首要言辭與其他指示所証明。若該要點遭駁斥，則整個哲學(梵經論)將不復為正確知識之手段，依據前述之原則，即詞語主要所指涉者即其意義。然而數論派之主要目的非伊濕瓦羅之否定，乃藉由身體與自我之區分而導達之自我所應獲得之最高對象。故儘管在否定伊濕瓦羅之部分遭取代，其仍將保有為正確知識之手段之地位，此復一次應依循該原則，即詞語主要所指涉者即其旨意。數論派因此具其適當領域，且僅在否定伊濕瓦羅此部分——即個人的、能動的神——而為易受攻擊者。
同樣不正確的是說在梵天‧彌曼差（Brahma-Mimamsa）中伊濕伐羅（Isvara）固然可以是主要對象，但卻不是其終極對象。
永恆的主權或神格。蓋如吠檀多前派（Pūrvapakṣa）所提之異議——針對彌曼差學派（Mimamsa）不重視其他傳承經典（Smṛti）之點——既不能成立，則顯然伊濕伐羅（Īśvara）唯有當被刻畫為具有永恆主權之特徵時，方能成為梵經彌曼差學（Brahma-Mimamsa）之對象。

若有人說《婆羅門‧彌曼差》的首部經句（Sūtra）並未說「現在有人希望認識最高的婆羅門」，因此該詞所指並非至高婆羅門（Parabrahman），我們不應因數論派（Samkhya）否定伊濕瓦羅（Iśvara）而推斷吠檀多與瑜伽體系亦只是指涉衍生的伊濕瓦羅（Karyeśvara，乃自性之產物）；倘若如此，則從第二卷第三章第一經起針對數論派的整個經句系列——證明無心之物質因其無法造化而不能僅憑推理而成立——便成為荒謬之談；因為若吠檀多之神乃造作而成或物質之產物，則數論派人士教導獨立物質（自性〔Prakṛti〕）之論便為正確。最後，伊濕瓦羅之永恆性自《瑜伽經》第一卷第二十六經甚為明白：「他（神）乃至古老聖賢之師，因其不受時間所限」，以及毗亞娑（Vyāsa）對此經句之注釋亦然。因此明白，數論派無非意欲否定伊濕瓦羅之常見俗流擬人化觀點，無論是作為一種退讓、還是作為一種大膽主張，或是出於其他原因，它與《婆羅門‧彌曼差》及瑜伽之間實不存有真實矛盾。
